base_method_text.title: LLM Post-Training: A Deep Dive into Reasoning Large Language Models
base_method_text.summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed the natural language processing
landscape and brought to life diverse applications. Pretraining on vast
web-scale data has laid the foundation for these models, yet the research
community is now increasingly shifting focus toward post-training techniques to
achieve further breakthroughs. While pretraining provides a broad linguistic
foundation, post-training methods enable LLMs to refine their knowledge,
improve reasoning, enhance factual accuracy, and align more effectively with
user intents and ethical considerations. Fine-tuning, reinforcement learning,
and test-time scaling have emerged as critical strategies for optimizing LLMs
performance, ensuring robustness, and improving adaptability across various
real-world tasks. This survey provides a systematic exploration of
post-training methodologies, analyzing their role in refining LLMs beyond
pretraining, addressing key challenges such as catastrophic forgetting, reward
hacking, and inference-time trade-offs. We highlight emerging directions in
model alignment, scalable adaptation, and inference-time reasoning, and outline
future research directions. We also provide a public repository to continually
track developments in this fast-evolving field:
https://github.com/mbzuai-oryx/Awesome-LLM-Post-training.
base_method_text.main_contributions: This survey provides a comprehensive review of post-training methodologies for Large Language Models (LLMs), covering fine-tuning, Reinforcement Learning (RL), and test-time scaling. It offers a structured taxonomy of post-training techniques, clarifies their roles and interconnections, and presents insights into open challenges and future research directions in optimizing LLMs for real-world deployment. The survey also provides practical guidance by introducing key benchmarks, datasets, and evaluation metrics essential for assessing post-training effectiveness.
base_method_text.methodology: The survey categorizes LLM post-training approaches into fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and test-time scaling. Fine-tuning adapts pre-trained LLMs to specific tasks using curated datasets. Reinforcement learning enhances adaptability by leveraging dynamic feedback and optimizing sequential decision-making. Test-time scaling optimizes model performance and efficiency without altering the core architecture. The survey also discusses various algorithms and techniques within each category, such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting.
base_method_text.experimental_setup: The survey references various benchmarks and datasets used to evaluate LLM post-training, covering reasoning tasks (e.g., MATH, GSM8K), alignment (e.g., HelpSteer, Anthropic HH-RLHF), multilingual evaluation (e.g., CulturaX), general comprehension (e.g., BigBench), and dialogue/search tasks (e.g., ConvAI2). It also mentions the use of both pointwise and pairwise evaluation criteria for assessing model performance across these tasks.
base_method_text.limitations: The survey highlights challenges such as catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning, reward hacking in reinforcement learning, the high cost and subjectivity of human feedback, and the difficulty of reliably orchestrating inference-time processes in test-time scaling. It also mentions the potential for data leakage and privacy risks during fine-tuning, as well as benchmark saturation issues.
base_method_text.future_research_directions: The survey suggests future research directions including exploring better continual learning strategies to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, developing robust AI feedback systems calibrated to human values, creating adaptive systems that dynamically allocate computation based on estimated query complexity, investigating hybrid reward models integrating process supervision with outcome-based rewards, and developing privacy-preserving personalization techniques. It also points to the need for collaborative multi-model systems and efficient RL training paradigms.
